title: Brother From Another Series

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes car in opening sequence
jokes.punchline: Classic Homer exclamation and scream
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer catchphrase, perfectly economical and character-specific, but not original within the show's context

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Inside every hardened criminal beats the heart of a 10-year-old boy. And vice versa.
jokes.setup: Marge's sweet sentiment about criminals having childlike hearts
jokes.punchline: Homer's disturbing reversal implying children are criminals
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of Marge's naive sentiment with perfect economy and dark humor that's quintessentially Homer

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Criminals are just like children, really. They have childlike hearts.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So you're saying children are criminals?

jokes.text: Yeah, maybe so, but they get the job done. Hey! Now I know where my tax dollars are going.
jokes.setup: Discussion about criminals not being cool
jokes.punchline: Homer's misguided approval of prison entertainment spending
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Homer's backwards priorities

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Criminals aren't cool.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, they should make prisons more fun. You know, add some water slides, maybe a lazy river...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not how prisons work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, they should! Taxpayer money should go to prisoner entertainment!

jokes.text: Well, Krusty, as you may remember, after I tried to frame you for armed robbery... I tried to murder Selma Bouvier. Let's see. I rigged the mayoral elections... I tried to blow up Springfield with a nuclear device, and I tried to kill you.
jokes.setup: Krusty asks what Bob has been up to
jokes.punchline: Bob casually lists increasingly heinous crimes
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating list delivered with Bob's characteristic eloquence, great callback structure

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, the usual. Been keeping busy with a little light murder, some arson, a dash of armed robbery, and I've been working on my interpretive dance routine.

jokes.text: And whenever I could find a spare moment, I've tried to murder... Bart Simpson. [Screaming] [Door Slamming] He said 'tried'!
jokes.setup: Bob casually mentions trying to kill Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's terrified reaction and Homer's unhelpful clarification
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy with Bart's panic and Homer's oblivious attempt to comfort

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You know, I've been trying to kill you for years.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Whoa! Whoa!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He means in chess, boy.

jokes.text: [Groans] Someone should really go up there and talk to him. [Groans] [Sighs] Homer! Ohh, all those stairs.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests someone should comfort Bart
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate complaint about having to climb stairs
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer laziness, well-integrated character moment

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, why don't you go comfort Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Aw, do I have to? He's all the way upstairs!

jokes.text: Sure, you're the one who ruined all of Sideshow Bob's criminal schemes. We're very proud of you, by the way. And, sure, he's probably so insane with rage... that he'd butcher you horribly if he could. But he's safely locked away. In a medium-security prison. For life! Unless he gets out somehow. Which is impossible! Or so you think. Except he's done it so many times before.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Bart about Bob
jokes.punchline: Each reassurance immediately undermined by worse reality
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalating structure where each comforting statement is immediately negated, perfect Homer obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, son. Bob's probably just in the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, he's dead.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, at least he had a nice funeral.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we cremated him in a dumpster.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The point is, Bart, he's in a better place now.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, we scattered his ashes in the sewage treatment plant.

jokes.text: Thanks for trying, guys. But Sideshow Bob wants me dead. And Sideshow Bob is an unstoppable killing machine.
jokes.punchline: Bart's matter-of-fact acceptance of his doom
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery, calling Bob an 'unstoppable killing machine' is both dramatic and absurd

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There, there, son. Everything's going to be okay.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No it's not, Dad. I'm doomed.

jokes.text: Really, Reverend, your praise is gonna get me beaten up.
jokes.setup: Reverend praising Bob in front of other prisoners
jokes.punchline: Bob's concern about prison social dynamics
jokes.explanation: Good prison humor showing Bob's intelligence about social dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Sideshow Bob, you've been such a good influence on our prison ministry.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Thank you, Reverend. Though I must admit, I'm worried the other inmates might think I'm getting special treatment.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: It could affect my standing in the prison hierarchy.

jokes.text: Yes, I'm a good, good man.
jokes.punchline: Overly emphatic self-praise from a serial killer
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective irony from known villain claiming goodness

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I've turned over a new leaf! I'm a changed man! Why, I haven't tried to kill anyone in... *checks watch* ...three whole days! I'm practically a saint!

jokes.text: You mean Arthur Fiedler's wake? No, no, no. I mean our falling out.
jokes.punchline: Cecil's assumption it's about a conductor's wake rather than their personal conflict
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment showing both brothers' pretentious cultural references

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about the unpleasantness between us.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Oh yes, a terrible tragedy. That conductor's wake was quite moving.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: No, I meant our personal conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Goodness, I had no idea. For, you see, I have been on Mars for the last decade... in a cave with my eyes shut and my fingers in my ears.
jokes.punchline: Cecil's elaborate sarcastic excuse for not knowing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating absurdity showing Cecil's wit and sophistication, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Oh, I had no idea you were a criminal! I must have been out of town the day they published that in the newspaper. Or perhaps I was distracted by my duties as the town's official record keeper, which surely would have required me to catalog such important information. How terribly negligent of me!

jokes.text: And blood is thicker than bread and water.
jokes.setup: Cecil explaining why he'll help Bob
jokes.punchline: Mangled version of 'blood is thicker than water'
jokes.explanation: Simple malapropism, functional but not especially clever

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Well, you see, Bob, blood is thicker than water. We're brothers, after all.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Or as I like to say, the blood of the covenant is thicker than the water of the womb... no wait, I had it backwards.

jokes.text: I'm all murdered out.
jokes.setup: Bob reassuring Cecil about his criminal past
jokes.punchline: Treating murder like a temporary hobby he's tired of
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical dark humor, treating murder casually like being 'all shopped out'

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Cecil, my dear brother, those days are behind me. Murder was just a phase—like my brief flirtation with macramé.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: You mean you're... reformed?

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Precisely. I've moved on to more constructive pursuits. Besides, killing gets so tedious after the first hundred or so.

jokes.text: Bob is no maniac. He explained his reasons for trying to kill us all. And I assure you, they were perfectly sane.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's misguided defense that his murder attempts were rational
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's intellectual approach even to murder attempts

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Bob's a maniac! Bob's a maniac!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, wait! You don't understand. Mr. Burns' murder attempts were completely rational given his socioeconomic position and the systematic inequalities that—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, shut up.

jokes.text: Uh, have the boys check into that.
jokes.setup: Bob asking if justice system is based on possibility of change
jokes.punchline: Quimby's clueless bureaucratic response to philosophical question
jokes.explanation: Perfect Quimby moment treating deep question as administrative task

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Change? I'm not familiar with that word. Is it a new type of currency?

jokes.text: And as an American, aren't I entitled to one? Probably!
jokes.setup: Bob's eloquent plea for a second chance
jokes.punchline: Quimby's uncertain agreement to basic American principle
jokes.explanation: Good political satire with Quimby's uncertainty about basic democratic values

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I beseech you. I have paid my debt to society. I am a changed man. I ask only for a second chance to prove that I can be a productive member of this community.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Well, Bob, that's a very moving speech. And I suppose everyone deserves a second chance. After all, that's what America is all about.

jokes.text: [Thinking] I hope they still make that shampoo I like.
jokes.setup: Bart worried about Bob's evil thoughts
jokes.punchline: Bob's mundane concern about hair care products
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of expectations - instead of evil plotting, Bob thinks about grooming, perfectly timed and economical

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sideshow Bob, I know you're thinking about doing something evil!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Actually, I was just wondering if I should switch to a moisturizing shampoo. My hair has been looking rather dull lately.

jokes.text: I have the '82 Château Latour and a rather indifferent Rauzan-Ségla.
jokes.punchline: Pretentious wine snobbery with casual dismissal of expensive wine
jokes.explanation: Perfect character establishment showing Cecil's pretentious sophistication

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: This is a 1982 Château Latour. A truly exceptional vintage.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Wow, that sounds fancy.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Indeed. Most people couldn't distinguish it from a box wine, of course.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I like box wine.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's precisely what I mean.

jokes.text: I'll be happy just as long as it doesn't taste like orange drink fermented under a radiator.
jokes.punchline: Vivid description of prison alcohol
jokes.explanation: Vivid, specific description contrasts Bob's sophistication with prison reality

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Wine? I prefer a nice Château d'Yquem. Though I suppose in prison, one makes do with toilet wine fermented in a sock.

jokes.text: And please, God, kill Sideshow Bob. It's him or me, O Lord!
jokes.punchline: Asking God to commit murder
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with child's innocent prayer turned murderous

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dear God, we paid for all this stuff ourselves, so thanks for nothing. And yeah, I'm gonna take some candy from the bowl at the front. Don't tell me you didn't see that one coming. Oh, and one more thing: Kill Flanders.

jokes.text: You can't ask God to kill someone! Yeah! You do your own dirty work.
jokes.punchline: Homer's worse advice to commit murder personally
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation where Homer's 'correction' is worse than the original problem, quintessential character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, you're supposed to say 'lead us not into temptation,' not 'lead us into a 7-Eleven.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: If you're gonna commit murder, you gotta do it yourself. None of this 'lead us' stuff.

jokes.text: Hydrological and hydrodynamical. Talk about running the gamut.
jokes.punchline: Bob's sarcastic comment about the redundant technical terms
jokes.explanation: Good intellectual humor showing Bob's wit and education

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh yes, Cecil, the 'Chief Hydraulic Pump Operator' — because 'Chief Hydraulic Pump Operator' wasn't redundant enough, we had to add 'Chief' to make it sound important.

jokes.text: What about the buffoon lessons? The four years at clown college?
jokes.setup: Bob listing Cecil's preparation for being Krusty's sidekick
jokes.punchline: Revealing clown college was Princeton
jokes.explanation: Excellent build-up to Princeton revelation, perfect character comedy about pretension vs. reality

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: He attended clown college.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Princeton.

jokes.text: I'll thank you not to refer to Princeton that way.
jokes.punchline: Reveal that clown college was actually Princeton
jokes.explanation: Masterful punchline that reframes everything - Princeton as 'clown college' is brilliant cultural satire, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I went to clown college.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Clown college?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Princeton.

jokes.text: These giant clown mitts are extremely hilarious, but do make it difficult to shift gears. That's the title of Erma Bombeck's latest, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Cecil's practical complaint about clown mitts
jokes.punchline: Bob's joke that it sounds like a domestic humor book title
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference humor showing both brothers' literary pretensions

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: These clown mitts make it impossible to do anything! I can't turn a doorknob, I can't pick up a phone...

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: That sounds like a self-help book title: 'Clown Mitts: Why I Can't Do Anything.'

jokes.text: Free comedy tip, slick. The pie gag's only funny when the sap's got dignity! Like that guy. Hey, Hal! Pie job for Lord Autumnbottom there!
jokes.setup: Krusty explaining why Cecil's pie bit failed
jokes.punchline: Targeting the dignified Bob instead
jokes.explanation: Good comedy theory delivered as Krusty wisdom, 'Lord Autumnbottom' is a great pretentious name

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: See, the problem with Cecil's pie bit is he was targeting the dignified Bob instead of some schnook like me.

jokes.text: That guy's a genius! He's gonna change the way we think about getting hit by pies!
jokes.punchline: Krusty's overwrought praise for pie-taking artistry
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor treating slapstick as high art

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, what you just witnessed was not merely a pie in the face—it was a masterclass in comedic timing, facial positioning, and the ancient art of pie-absorption! This man is a virtuoso, a Leonardo da Vinci of the custard arts! He took that pie with the grace of a swan, the dignity of a prince, and the splatter radius of a watermelon dropped from the Empire State Building!

jokes.text: Cecil, no civilization in history has ever considered... chief hydrological engineer a calling. [Clearing Throat] Yes, yes. The Cappadocians. Fine.
jokes.setup: Bob dismissing engineering as a calling
jokes.punchline: Cecil's immediate obscure historical correction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy showing both brothers' obscure knowledge and competitive one-upmanship

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Engineering? That's not a calling, that's just a job.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Actually, brother, the term 'calling' in the occupational sense dates back to the Protestant work ethic as articulated by Max Weber in his 1905 seminal work 'The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism.'

jokes.text: Just the thought of that raw, surging power makes me wonder why the hell I should care.
jokes.punchline: Bob's complete disinterest after seeming to share the excitement
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Bob seemingly sharing enthusiasm then revealing apathy

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, hydroelectric power is quite fascinating.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Isn't it wonderful, brother? The way the water flows through the turbines, generating clean energy for thousands!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Mmm, yes, quite riveting. Now, if you'll excuse me, I have some rather pressing matters to attend to.

jokes.text: Oh, great. Whenever a woman passes by... I suppose it will be my job to lead the hooting. Oh, yeah! Shake it, madam. Capital knockers.
jokes.setup: Bob complaining about supervising construction workers
jokes.punchline: Parodying crude catcalls in his refined vocabulary
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Bob's refined vocabulary applied to crude construction worker behavior, 'capital knockers' is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Good heavens, the uncouth remarks these construction workers direct at passing pedestrians! 'Hey baby, what's shakin'?' Preposterous. If one is to engage in such pedestrian flirtation, one ought to employ proper diction.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: For instance: 'I say, what a positively resplendent derrière you possess! Might I have the honor of its acquaintance?'

jokes.text: Maybe he's gonna pee in the river. Mmm, nah. That's not his style.
jokes.setup: Bart wondering what Bob's evil plan is
jokes.punchline: Recognizing that simple vandalism isn't sophisticated enough for Bob
jokes.explanation: Good character insight showing Bart understands Bob's sophisticated criminal mind

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's Bob planning this time? It's gotta be something big.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nah, simple vandalism? That's kid stuff. Bob's way too twisted for that.

jokes.text: He's just shy because I've tried to kill him so many times.
jokes.punchline: Bob's casual explanation of attempted murders as shyness
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery treating murder attempts as minor social awkwardness, economical and darkly funny

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: You seem nervous, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, I'm about to meet Sideshow Bob for the first time.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Ah, don't be shy, my boy. I've only tried to kill you a few times. It's nothing personal—just a bit of nervousness on my part.

jokes.text: Oh, the pleasure's mine, Bob. It's so exciting to be dating a killer. To be fair, I never successfully killed anyone. Oh. I see.
jokes.setup: Edna excited about dating a killer
jokes.punchline: Her disappointment when Bob clarifies his lack of success
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Edna's disappointment at Bob's incompetence as a killer, good character moment

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: Oh Sideshow Bob, I'm so thrilled to be dating a killer!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I've never successfully killed anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: I did once try to kill the world's greatest lover. But then I realized there are laws against suicide.
jokes.setup: Bob claiming to have tried to kill the world's greatest lover
jokes.punchline: Revealing it would be suicide because he considers himself the greatest lover
jokes.explanation: Masterful setup and punchline showing Bob's narcissism through perfectly structured joke, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I tried to kill the world's greatest lover.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: It would be suicide.

jokes.text: Well, that's the last time I announce my dinner plans in class.
jokes.punchline: Edna's practical concern about classroom oversharing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Edna's teacher perspective on the situation

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: I can't have you telling the other children about my personal life. They'll lose all respect for me.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Mrs. Krabappel, they already don't respect you.

dialogue.speaker: Edna
dialogue.line: Well, what little they have left, I'd like to keep.

jokes.text: He says that, but I bet he gives up pretty quickly.
jokes.setup: Bart's vow to watch Bob constantly
jokes.punchline: Bob's confident dismissal (which proves wrong)
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony since audience knows Bart won't give up, sets up later payoff

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Ha! You'll never catch me, Bart Simpson! I'm far too clever for your constant vigilance!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: We'll see about that, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Pfft. I laugh in the face of your surveillance! By tomorrow, I'll be long gone, and you'll realize what a fool you've been!

jokes.text: You speak as if they were a gaggle of slack-jawed yokels. Mr. Terwilliger, come quick! There's trouble down to the cement mixer, sir! See, Cousin Merl and me was playing fetch with Geech. That's our old smellhound and- Geech gone to heaven, Mr. Terwilliger.
jokes.setup: Cecil defending his workers from Bob's insults
jokes.punchline: Immediate proof they are exactly slack-jawed yokels
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing with immediate contradiction of Cecil's defense, classic Cletus characterization

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Now see here, Bob, my workers are honest, hard-working people!

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Uh, what's a hard-working?

jokes.text: Temper, temper. You know Cousin Merl ain't been quite right lately.
jokes.punchline: Understating obvious mental problems as being 'not quite right'
jokes.explanation: Good understatement humor typical of rural character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Well, Cousin Merl ain't quite right.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'not quite right'? He's eatin' the dirt!

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Yeah, but other than that...

jokes.text: Coveralls that don't quite cover all.
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'coveralls' garment name
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective wordplay, very economical

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: These working conditions are absolutely intolerable! Do you know what they expect me to wear? Coveralls!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Coveralls! As if I'm supposed to cover... all of myself? Preposterous!

jokes.text: Sometimes I wish this dam would burst... and bury this cursed town.
jokes.punchline: Casual wish for town's destruction (which becomes plot point)
jokes.explanation: Good foreshadowing disguised as complaint, typical Bob murderous thinking

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'm so tired of this job, the constant ridicule, the thankless work... Sometimes I just wish this whole town would burn to the ground!

jokes.text: Hey! You said we were going to Dairy Queen. I lied. Now help me rummage through Bob's trash for clues. Then I promise we'll go to the waterslide. Okay.
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual admission of lying and new bribe offer
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's manipulation of Milhouse

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Bart, where are we going? I thought you said we were going to Dairy Queen!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, I lied.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: You lied?!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Look, I'll buy you a pretzel.

jokes.text: Madam, your children are no more... than a pair of ill-bred troublemakers.
jokes.setup: Bob's ominous announcement about the children
jokes.punchline: Anticlimatic complaint instead of death announcement
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with threatening setup leading to mundane complaint

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'm going to kill the children.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Just kidding. I'm going to complain about their noise.

jokes.text: Well, I hope Bob fed you 'cause I ate your dinners.
jokes.punchline: Homer's selfish concern about dinner
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer selfishness, good character moment but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Where have you been? I've been waiting hours for dinner!

jokes.text: Bob's trailer at the construction site? That's even better. Let's go there. What were you thinking? The haunted mine.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting Bob's trailer for evidence
jokes.punchline: Bart's absurd alternative of haunted mine
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic from Bart, shows different thinking styles

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We could check Bob's trailer for evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Or we could search the haunted mine where the ghost of a prospector guards his gold.

jokes.text: If I didn't know better, I'd swear Bob was building a dam.
jokes.punchline: Stating the obvious as if it's suspicious
jokes.explanation: Simple irony but not particularly clever, functional plot beat

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... this is a blueprint. For a building. And look — it has walls. Walls that are... rectangular.

jokes.text: Who left the lights on? Who's in here? Cletus? Cousin Merl? Big HungryJoe?
jokes.setup: Cecil discovering someone in his trailer
jokes.punchline: List of yokel workers including 'Big HungryJoe'
jokes.explanation: 'Big HungryJoe' is a great absurd character name in the list

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Well, well, well... what do we have here?

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Let's see... we got Smilin' Pete, Toothless Jake, Big Hungry Joe, Ol' One-Eyed Jimbo, and the Bibulous Barney.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Looks like the whole dang crew decided to use my trailer as a meetin' hall!

jokes.text: Be careful! There's hydroelectricity in there!
jokes.punchline: Treating hydroelectricity like a substance rather than a process
jokes.explanation: Good technical humor showing Cecil's engineer mindset applied incorrectly

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Warning: hydroelectric dam malfunction imminent. All residents must evacuate immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hydroelectric? Sounds like some kinda drug. I'm in!

jokes.text: Then you must have had your eyes closed when you embezzled it!
jokes.setup: Bob claiming he's never seen the money
jokes.punchline: Bart's literal interpretation of not seeing while committing crime
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay showing Bart's logical thinking

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I've never seen that money!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh, so you committed the crime with your eyes closed?

jokes.text: Here's a clue for you, Jack! One hundred thousand tons of reinforced concrete.
jokes.setup: Bob offering to explain what he's been doing
jokes.punchline: Dramatic reveal that's just his legitimate job
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Bob's dramatic presentation of mundane fact

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You want to know what I've been doing all this time? Very well, I shall tell you.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I've been working as an accountant at a mid-sized firm in Springfield.

jokes.text: I know Cousin Merl has had his troubles with the revenuers, but he's hardly a master-
jokes.punchline: Cletus thinking they mean Cousin Merl
jokes.explanation: Good character humor with Cletus's limited worldview and moonshining reference

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: A master criminal? Why, that'd be my Cousin Merl!

jokes.text: Off the record, yes. But officially, I did it for the money!
jokes.setup: Bob asking if it's about the sidekick job
jokes.punchline: Cecil's contradictory admission of both motives
jokes.explanation: Good villain moment showing Cecil's honesty about his petty motivations

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Well, it's both. I want the job AND I want to destroy you.

jokes.text: When it's one of my schemes, you can't foil it fast enough. But when Cecil tries to kill you... 'It's hopeless. Utterly, utterly hopeless.'
jokes.punchline: Bob's hurt feelings about Bart's different attitude toward his schemes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor with Bob showing professional jealousy and hurt pride about his criminal reputation

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I've devoted my life to schemes against that boy, and now he treats me like I'm just some common criminal!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: We had something special, Bart and I. A bond. An understanding.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: But now? Now he won't even return my calls. He blocks my number!

jokes.text: Come on! Quickly now! Bart, for once I'm not trying to kill you. I know it's awkward and I don't like it any more than you do. But you've got to trust me.
jokes.punchline: Bob acknowledging how weird it is for both of them
jokes.explanation: Great character moment acknowledging their unusual relationship dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Well Bart, I must say, this is quite awkward. Here I am, saving your life, when for years I've devoted myself to ending it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, Bob, this is pretty weird.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Indeed. Perhaps we're not so different after all, you and I.

jokes.text: Ah, what the hell.
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual acceptance of trusting his would-be killer
jokes.explanation: Perfect economical reaction showing Bart's pragmatic attitude

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I'm asking you to trust me.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay.

jokes.text: Let's go again! Let's go again! No!
jokes.punchline: Bart wanting to repeat the dangerous experience for fun
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's love of danger even in crisis

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That was awesome! Let's do it again!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, you almost died.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Exactly! Again!

jokes.text: Lisa, you don't spend 10 years as a homicidal maniac... without learning a few things about dynamite.
jokes.setup: Lisa asking if Bob knows what he's doing
jokes.punchline: Bob's matter-of-fact explanation of his explosive expertise
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob line treating homicidal mania as professional experience, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Do you know what you're doing?

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh yes, Lisa. You see, I have a degree in explosives from a very prestigious correspondence school.

jokes.text: Guess who! Maris?
jokes.punchline: Cecil's sophisticated guess referencing Frasier
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Cecil's cultural sophistication even under attack

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Guess who?

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Is it someone of refined taste and intelligence? Someone who appreciates the finer things in life? Someone who might enjoy a glass of Chateau Margaux while discussing Proust?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Nope, it's me.

jokes.text: Ooh, I suppose it is. Huh. If anyone asks, I'll lie.
jokes.setup: Bart pointing out murder method is crude
jokes.punchline: Cecil's casual agreement and solution
jokes.explanation: Good villain humor showing Cecil's casual attitude toward murder and deception

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's a pretty crude way to commit murder.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: You're right. I'll use poison.

jokes.text: There may be a slight ringing in your ears. Fortunately, you'll be nowhere near them.
jokes.punchline: Polite warning followed by death threat punchline
jokes.explanation: Masterful dark humor with polite warning structure hiding death threat, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Well, I'm about to detonate these explosives. I should probably warn everyone.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Ahem. Everybody, I'm going to blow up the dam in five minutes. Please evacuate in an orderly fashion.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: And if anyone interferes with my plans, I will kill you.

jokes.text: Bart, how would you like to do something incredibly noble? Do we have to? Yes.
jokes.punchline: Bart's reluctant acceptance of nobility
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's reluctance toward heroism

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I shall throw myself from a cliff to save you, boy!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, thanks Bob. That's actually pretty noble of you.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Yes, well, don't get used to it.

jokes.text: Oh, I don't know about that. [Whimpers] Joking. Joking.
jokes.setup: Bart saying Bob can't try to kill him anymore
jokes.punchline: Bob's instinctive threat followed by quick clarification
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Bob can't help his murderous instincts

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'll kill you!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: ...I mean, I'll sue you!

jokes.text: Oh, I don't relish having to write the Christmas letter this year. Nor I to read it.
jokes.setup: Cecil being arrested for his crimes
jokes.punchline: Both brothers' concern about family Christmas correspondence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant upper-class concern about maintaining family appearances despite criminal activities

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose this means we won't be exchanging Christmas cards this year.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, we'll exchange them, Cecil. I'll just be reading mine through prison bars.

jokes.text: That sounds like the testimony of crazy old Lisa Simpson.
jokes.punchline: Wiggum dismissing child's accurate testimony
jokes.explanation: Functional Wiggum moment but predictable pattern

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Chief Wiggum, the evidence clearly shows that—

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now, now, Simpson girl. I've been a cop for a long time, and I've learned one thing: kids don't know anything about nothing.

jokes.text: That's some good work, Lou. You'll make sergeant for this. Uh, I already am sergeant, Chief.
jokes.punchline: Wiggum not knowing Lou's rank
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence callback, well-executed but familiar

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Lou, you're a fine officer. One of the best on the force.

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Thanks, Chief. I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: In fact, I'm promoting you to... uh... what rank are you now?

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: I'm a sergeant, Chief.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Right, right. Well, you're still promoted.

jokes.text: Quiet, Lou... or I will bust you down to sergeant so fast it'll make your head spin.
jokes.setup: Lou pointing out he's already sergeant
jokes.punchline: Wiggum threatening to demote him to his current rank
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Wiggum still doesn't understand the rank system

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You're right, Lou. Tell you what - I'll demote you to sergeant.

jokes.text: Quiet, Lou... or I will bust you down to sergeant so fast it'll make your head spin.
jokes.punchline: Wiggum threatening to demote Lou to his current rank
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier confusion about Lou's rank - Wiggum's consistent incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Chief, I think you mean 'sergeant', not 'lieutenant'.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, you're demoted to Chief of Police!

jokes.text: I think I wet my bed.
jokes.punchline: Homer's childish fear reaction
jokes.explanation: Simple toilet humor, fits character but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is it so dark? Did I go blind? Am I blind?!

jokes.text: You'll live to regret this! Oh, thanks a lot. Now I look crazy.
jokes.setup: Wiggum telling Bob to threaten them
jokes.punchline: Bob's frustration at being made to look unhinged
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor with Bob being self-aware about how villain threats make him look crazy

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, very well. I'll threaten them. But I want it on record that I find this whole thing degrading!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Just do it, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Listen here, you simpletons! I'm going to— Oh, what's the use? You've already made me look like a complete lunatic!

jokes.text: I'm older! I get the top bunk! Oh, poppycock! I called it at the arraignment! Get back!
jokes.punchline: Sophisticated criminals fighting over bunks like children
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending showing that despite their sophistication, they're petty siblings at heart

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Cecil, I insist the upper bunk is mine. I require the superior vantage point for my criminal machinations.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Absolutely not, Robert. As the elder brother, I claim it by right.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You shan't have it! I saw it first!

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Did not!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Did too!

jokes.text: So, when do they bring us the menus?
jokes.punchline: Cecil's expectation of restaurant-style service in prison
jokes.explanation: Good final character moment showing Cecil's continued obliviousness to reality

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: I expect restaurant-style service in here!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You're in prison, Cecil.

dialogue.speaker: Cecil
dialogue.line: Yes, but I'm a paying customer!

tmdb_overview: When Sideshow Bob is released from prison, his brother Cecil puts him in charge of building Springfield's new hydroelectric dam.
insight: Sideshow Bob's twin unleashes 71 jokes in 22 minutes — Simpsons' densest villain episode ever.
